title: Shamanic Crisis and Kundalini: The Overlap Between Indigenous and Eastern Frameworks
meta_description: You're probably here because you're scared. You've been through something – or you're going through it right now – that doesn't fit into any normal box. Yo
body:
Shamanic Crisis and Kundalini: The Overlap Between Indigenous and Eastern Frameworks

You're probably here because you're scared. You've been through something - or you're going through it right now - that doesn't fit into any normal box. You feel like you're losing your mind, or maybe you've already lost it. Your body is doing weird things, your thoughts are racing, and you're seeing or hearing stuff that no one else does. You're alone, aren't you? It's 3 AM, and you're staring at the ceiling, wondering if you'll ever be "normal" again. I know that feeling. I've been there. That crushing isolation, the terror that you've broken something inside yourself that can't be fixed. It’s a special kind of hell, isn't it?

You've probably stumbled across terms like "kundalini awakening" or "spiritual emergency." Maybe someone mentioned "shamanic sickness." And you're trying to figure out what the hell is happening to you. You're looking for answers, for something that makes sense of the chaos. And you're finding these two seemingly separate worlds - the ancient wisdom of indigenous cultures and the esoteric practices of the East - talking about experiences that sound eerily similar to yours. It’s confusing, I get it. But there's a reason for that overlap, and understanding it might just give you a sliver of peace, or at least a direction to move in. You're not crazy, you're just undergoing something intense. Right?

I'm not gonna lie to you, it's not easy. What you're going through, it's a deep, raw process. But here's the thing: you're not the first. And you won't be the last. People have been going through these kinds of transformations for thousands of years. They just had different names for it, different ways of understanding it, and critically, different ways of integrating it. That's what we're gonna talk about. The terrifying, bewildering, and ultimately, potentially deeply life-changing journey you're on.

The Terror of the Unexplainable: What You're Feeling

Let's talk about what's probably happening to you. Your body might feel like it's vibrating, buzzing, or burning. You might have involuntary movements, tremors, or sensations of energy moving through you. Your mind? It's probably a whirlwind. Racing thoughts, intense emotions, visions, sounds, even smells that aren't there. You might feel disconnected from your body, or like your sense of self is dissolving. You're probably having trouble sleeping, eating, and just generally functioning. Sound familiar? I thought so. It's not just "stress" or "anxiety," is it? It feels deeper, more primal. Like something ancient is stirring within you.

This is where the terms "shamanic crisis" and "kundalini awakening" start to merge in your mind. Both describe a sudden, often unbidden, and deeply disruptive eruption of inner energy or spiritual experience. It's not something you planned for, is it? You didn't sign up for this. It just hit you, like a freight train, and now you're trying to pick up the pieces.

Kundalini: The Serpent Uncoiling

In the Eastern traditions, particularly yoga and Tantra, kundalini is seen as a dormant spiritual energy coiled at the base of the spine, like a sleeping serpent. When it awakens, it's supposed to rise through the chakras, purifying the system and leading to spiritual enlightenment. Sounds great on paper, right? But what they don't always tell you in those glossy yoga magazines is that the awakening can be incredibly violent and disorienting. It's not always peaceful bliss and cosmic love. For many, it's a nightmare.

I've seen it. I've lived it. The intense heat, the uncontrollable shaking, the overwhelming fear, the feeling of your brain being rewired. You might feel like you're literally being electrocuted from the inside out. Your emotions can swing wildly from ecstatic joy to suicidal despair, often within minutes. You might have visions of deities, demons, past lives, or just pure, unadulterated chaos. Your senses can become incredibly heightened, making the normal world unbearable. You might not be able to tolerate certain foods, sounds, or even light. It's a full-body, full-mind assault. The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link) is a classic for a reason - it lays out the physical and psychological symptoms in blunt detail, and it’s a relief to read that someone else has felt what you’re feeling. And The Kundalini Guide by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) also gives you practical advice on how to manage this beast. You're not alone in this, truly. There are others who've walked this fire.

Shamanic Sickness: A Call to Power

Now, let's look at the shamanic side. Many indigenous cultures describe something called "shamanic sickness" or "shamanic initiation sickness." This isn't a disease in the conventional sense, but rather a deep, often debilitating, spiritual crisis that serves as a calling to become a shaman. It's a period of intense suffering, often involving physical illness, psychological breakdown, visions, dreams, and encounters with spirits. The person feels like they're dying, being torn apart, or going insane. They might wander off, become withdrawn, or behave in ways that seem erratic to their community.

Here's the twist: in these cultures, this isn't seen as a problem to be medicated away. It's seen as a sacred process. It's a sign that the individual has been chosen by the spirits, that they have a special destiny. The suffering isn't meaningless; it's a purification, a dismantling of the old self to make way for a new, more powerful one. The community and elders often support the individual through this process, guiding them, interpreting their visions, and teaching them how to work with the energies they're experiencing. They don't just leave you to rot in your terror. Know what I mean? They recognize it as a path, however brutal.

The Overlap: Why They Feel So Similar

So, you're probably seeing the parallels now. Both kundalini awakening and shamanic crisis involve:

  • Intense Physical Symptoms: Burning, shaking, tremors, changes in body temperature, pain, spontaneous movements.
  • deep Psychological Distress: Anxiety, fear, depression, derealization, depersonalization, psychosis-like states, feelings of impending doom or death.
  • Altered States of Consciousness: Visions, auditory experiences, out-of-body experiences, heightened intuition, psychic phenomena.
  • Disruption of Normal Life: Inability to work, maintain relationships, or perform daily tasks.
  • A Sense of Being Chosen or Called: Even if you don't believe in spirits or deities, there's often a feeling that something bigger than you is at play.
  • A Process of Death and Rebirth: The old self is dismantled, and a new, often more expanded, sense of self emerges (if you make it through).

The core experience, honestly, is often indistinguishable. Whether you call it "kundalini rising" or "shamanic sickness," you're going through a massive energetic and psychological reorganization. Your nervous system is overwhelmed, your perception of reality is shattered, and your ego is being put through the wringer. It's terrifying because it feels like you're losing control, and you are. But here's the thing: sometimes, losing control is exactly what's needed for something new to emerge. It doesn't make it any less scary, but it gives it purpose.

Look, the difference often lies in the cultural lens through which it's interpreted and, critically, the support system available. In Western society, if you describe these symptoms to a doctor, you're likely to get a psychiatric diagnosis - psychosis, bipolar disorder, severe anxiety. And while some of those diagnoses might be accurate on a symptomatic level, they often miss the spiritual dimension entirely. They don't offer a approach for understanding it as a potentially life-changing process, just a disease to be suppressed. That's a huge problem for you, because you're not just sick; you're undergoing something ancient. You're getting a call, even if it's a terrifying one.

The Missing Piece: Integration and Guidance

This is where indigenous traditions and even the original Eastern teachings had a massive advantage. They didn't just label it "crazy." They had systems, rituals, and experienced guides to help people through these crises. They understood that these experiences, while painful, could lead to deep wisdom and healing abilities. They provided a context, a community, and a path forward.

You, on the other hand, are probably feeling isolated, misunderstood, and frankly, like you're going insane. There's no shaman in your neighborhood to guide you, no elder to interpret your visions. You're left to piece it together yourself, often in secret, for fear of being institutionalized or ostracized. That's why books like Spiritual Emergency: When Personal Transformation Becomes a Crisis (paid link) by Stanislav and Christina Grof are so vital. They were among the first to really bring these concepts into Western psychology, recognizing that not all "psychosis" is pathology. Some of it is a spiritual breakthrough trying to happen. And The Stormy Search for the Self by Stanislav & Christina Grof (paid link) continues that work, giving you a wider lens to view your own internal storm. They give you language for what you're experiencing, and that alone can be a lifeline.

Why Integration Matters More Than Suppression

When you suppress these energies or experiences - through medication alone, or by simply trying to ignore them - they don't just go away. They often get stuck, creating chronic physical or mental health issues. The energy needs to move, to be processed, to be integrated. Think of it like a river that's trying to flow, but you've put a dam in front of it. The pressure builds, and eventually, something's gonna break. You don't want that. You want to learn how to guide the river, even if it feels like a raging torrent right now.

Integration means finding ways to incorporate these intense experiences into your daily life. It means understanding what they're trying to tell you, learning to work with the energy, and finding a new way to be in the world. It's not about going back to who you were before; that person is gone. It's about becoming who you're meant to be, scars and all. It's about taking the shattered pieces and forging something new, something stronger. Does that land? Because it's not about fixing you, it's about making you whole, in a completely new way.

Practical Steps When You're In It

Okay, so you're in the thick of it. What do you actually do? I'm not going to tell you to just "meditate more" or "think positive thoughts." That's dismissive bullshit when you're feeling like you're being torn apart. Here's what I've learned, what I've seen help others, and what makes some sense when nothing else does:

  • Grounding, Grounding, Grounding: This is your lifeline. When you feel like you're floating away, or your energy is too intense, you need to bring yourself back to earth.
    • Physical grounding: Walk barefoot on the grass. Touch a tree. Take a cold shower. Eat heavy, nourishing foods (root vegetables are great). Drink plenty of water. Focus on your breath, really feeling your belly rise and fall.
    • Sensory grounding: Focus on one sense at a time. What do you see? What do you hear? What do you smell? What do you taste? What do you feel on your skin? This pulls you out of the internal maelstrom.
  • Movement: This energy needs an outlet. If you can, move your body. Gentle yoga, walking, dancing, shaking. Don't force anything, but let your body express what it needs to. Sometimes, just shaking your hands and feet vigorously can help release some of the intense energy.
  • Find Safe Spaces and People: This is crucial. You need someone who believes you, who doesn't dismiss what you're experiencing as "crazy." It might be a therapist who understands spiritual emergency, a spiritual teacher, or even just a trusted friend or family member who can hold space for you without judgment. You might have to search for this person, but don't give up. Community, even a small one, is vital.
  • Journaling: Write down everything. Your visions, your fears, your physical sensations, your dreams. Don't filter it. Just let it pour out. This helps you process it, track patterns, and gives you a sense of agency over the chaos. It's a way of externalizing some of the internal pressure.
  • Limit Stimuli: Bright lights, loud noises, chaotic environments - they're probably overwhelming you. Reduce your exposure. Spend time in nature if you can. Create a calm, quiet space for yourself.
  • Ritual (Even Small Ones): Whether you're religious or not, simple rituals can be incredibly grounding. A daily prayer, lighting a candle, making an offering (even just a flower or a cup of water) to something you consider sacred. These acts create structure and meaning in a chaotic world.
  • Educate Yourself: Read books like the ones I mentioned. Read accounts of others who've gone through similar experiences. Knowledge is power, and knowing you're not alone, that there's a name for this, can be a huge comfort.
  • Professional Help: Don't dismiss therapy or even medication if you need it to stabilize. Sometimes, a temporary course of medication can bring enough relief to allow you to do the deeper integration work. The goal isn't to suppress, but to manage symptoms so you can function and heal. A therapist who understands spiritual emergency can be invaluable.

The Long Road Home (Which Isn't Home Anymore)

You're not going back to who you were. Let go of that idea. This experience has changed you, at its core. It's like you've been taken apart and put back together differently. And that's okay, even if it doesn't feel okay right now. The "you" that emerges from this will be more sensitive, more aware, and hopefully, more compassionate. You'll have a deeper understanding of suffering, both your own and others'. You'll see the world through new eyes.

The journey through shamanic crisis or kundalini awakening isn't a straight line. There will be good days and bad days, breakthroughs and setbacks. You'll feel like you're making progress, then suddenly you'll be plunged back into the depths of despair. That's normal. It's a process of unraveling and reweaving. Be patient with yourself. Be kind to yourself. You're doing incredibly difficult work, even if it just feels like survival.

So yeah, it's terrifying. It's lonely. It's everything you never wanted. But you're here, you're reading this, and that means you're fighting. You're seeking understanding. And that's the first step. Keep fighting. Keep seeking. You'll get through this, changed, but stronger. You'll find your way. I promise you.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
